hero.title: AI-Powered CVE Intelligence
hero.subtitle: NezhaCyberMCP
hero.description: Connect your AI assistant to real-time vulnerability data from CIRCL, GitHub Advisory, and MyCERT. Query CVEs, analyze threats, and generate security reports — all through natural language.

features.title: Everything your AI needs to reason about vulnerabilities
features.subtitle: A complete MCP server purpose-built for cybersecurity intelligence

realtime.title: Real-Time Sync
realtime.desc: Automatically syncs CVE data from CIRCL, GitHub Advisory Database, and MyCERT on a scheduled cron job.

search.title: Semantic Search
search.desc: Search across all three data sources simultaneously by keyword, severity, vendor, product, or CWE ID.

lambda.title: AWS Lambda Ready
lambda.desc: Detects runtime environment automatically. Runs as a long-lived MCP server locally or as a Lambda function on AWS.

secure.title: Credential Safety
secure.desc: All sensitive values are masked in logs. AWS credentials, DB passwords, and API keys never appear in output.

prompts.title: Built-in Prompts
prompts.desc: Pre-built prompt templates for security advisories, patch prioritization, stakeholder triage, and weekly reports.

db.title: Multi-DB Support
db.desc: Supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, QuestDB, and Amazon Aurora DSQL via GORM.

sources.title: Three authoritative data sources
sources.subtitle: Aggregated, deduplicated, and always up to date

circl.name: CIRCL CVE
circl.desc: Computer Incident Response Center Luxembourg — the authoritative CVE feed with full CVSS scoring and affected package data.

github.name: GitHub Advisory
github.desc: GitHub's curated security advisory database covering npm, PyPI, Maven, Go, Rust, and more ecosystems.

mycert.name: MyCERT
mycert.desc: Malaysia Computer Emergency Response Team — regional security bulletins covering threats relevant to Southeast Asia.

tools.title: MCP Tools
tools.subtitle: Ask your AI assistant to call these tools directly

items.get_cve: Look up a specific CVE by ID across all three data sources
items.search_cves: Full-text search with filters for severity, vendor, product, CWE, and date range
items.whats_new: Get the latest CVEs published after a given date
items.filter_severity: Filter CVEs by CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW severity
items.bulk_get: Retrieve multiple CVEs in a single call
items.vuln_trends: Analyze vulnerability trends over time by severity
items.search_by_cpe: Search CVEs by CPE (Common Platform Enumeration) string
items.related_cves: Find CVEs related to a given CVE by shared CWE or affected packages

cta.title: Ready to give your AI superpowers?
cta.desc: Add NezhaCyberMCP to your Claude, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible AI assistant. No database setup required — CVE data is served directly from our infrastructure.
cta.config_title: Add to your MCP config — that's it

install.title: Install & Configure Your AI Toolkit
install.desc: Step-by-step guide to set up Trae, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and NezhaCyberMCP on your machine.

s1.title: Prerequisites
s1.desc: Ensure your system meets the minimum requirements before proceeding.

s2.title: Trae IDE
s2.desc: Trae is an AI-native IDE built for agentic coding workflows. Install it as your primary development environment.
s2.step1_desc: Run the official install script. On macOS/Linux this installs the Trae binary and adds it to your PATH.
s2.step2_desc: Open Trae and sign in with your account. Trae supports GitHub OAuth and email login.
s2.step2_note: Trae is free for individual developers. No credit card required.
s2.step3_desc: Use File → Open Folder to open your NezhaCyberMCP directory. Trae will auto-detect the Go workspace.
s2.verify: Launch Trae from your Applications folder or terminal. The welcome screen should appear with your account name in the top-right corner.

s3.title: Claude Code
s3.desc: Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding CLI. It runs in your terminal and can read, write, and execute code autonomously.
s3.step1_desc: Install the Claude Code CLI globally using npm. Requires Node.js 18 LTS or later.
s3.step2_desc: Log in with your Anthropic account. This stores a session token in ~/.claude.
s3.step3_desc: Register NezhaCyberMCP as an MCP server so Claude can call its tools.
s3.step3_note: You need an active Anthropic API subscription (Pro or Team) to use Claude Code.
s3.verify: Run `claude --version` in your terminal. You should see a version string like `1.x.x`.

s4.title: OpenAI Codex
s4.desc: OpenAI Codex CLI is a terminal-based AI coding agent powered by GPT-4o. It supports MCP tool integration.
s4.step1_desc: Install the Codex CLI globally. Requires Node.js 18+ and an OpenAI API key.
s4.step2_desc: Export your OpenAI API key as an environment variable. Add this to your shell profile for persistence.
s4.step2_note: Never commit your API key to version control. Use a .env file or a secrets manager.
s4.step3_desc: Run `codex` in your project directory. Codex will read your codebase and await instructions.
s4.verify: Run codex --version to confirm the installation. Expected output: openai/codex x.x.x.

s5.title: NezhaCyberMCP
s5.desc: Clone, build, and wire up the MCP server binary to your AI assistant.
s5.step1_desc: Clone NezhaCyberMCP from GitHub and navigate into the project directory.
s5.step2_desc: Run `make build` to compile the Go binary. The output binary is named `advisory`.
s5.step3_desc: Add the config block below to your AI assistant's MCP configuration file (e.g. ~/.claude/mcp.json or Trae's MCP settings).
s5.step3_note: The `./advisory` path must be the absolute or relative path to the compiled binary on your machine.
s5.step4_desc: Run `make run` to start the MCP server. It will begin syncing CVE data in the background.
s5.verify: Open your AI assistant and ask: 'What are the latest CRITICAL CVEs?' — it should call the `whats_new` tool and return real data.

s6.title: Troubleshooting
s6.desc: Common issues and how to resolve them.
s6.q1: `make build` fails with 'go: command not found'
s6.a1: Go is not installed or not in your PATH. Install Go from golang.org/dl and ensure `go` is accessible.
s6.a2: The binary is not executable. Run the command below to fix permissions.
s6.q3: Claude Code shows 'MCP server not connected'
s6.a3: Ensure the `command` path in your mcp.json points to the correct absolute path of the `advisory` binary. Relative paths may not resolve correctly depending on how Claude Code is launched.
s6.q4: How do I add NezhaCyberMCP to Claude Code via CLI?
s6.a4: Use the `claude mcp add` command to register the server. Replace the path with your actual binary location.

footer.tagline: Built with Go · Powered by MCP · Open Source
